section.title: Solomon's Wisdom
section.sectionRationale: With the throne secured, chapter 3 turns to Solomon's reign itself, opening with a note of compromise, an alliance sealed by marriage to Pharaoh's daughter and worship still offered at the high places because the temple is not yet built. At Gibeon the LORD appears to Solomon in a dream and offers him anything he asks; Solomon, aware of his own inexperience, requests an understanding heart to govern God's people rather than long life, wealth, or victory over his enemies. God grants the request and adds riches and honor besides. The chapter closes with Solomon's famous judgment between two women disputing a living child, proof before all Israel that the wisdom of God was in him.
section.thesis: Solomon's request at Gibeon for an understanding heart, rather than long life, wealth, or vengeance, delights the LORD, who grants him unmatched wisdom along with riches and honor he did not ask for, and that wisdom is immediately displayed before all Israel in a judgment that reveals a true mother's heart and establishes Solomon as a king in whom the wisdom of God visibly dwells.
section.frameNote: Four beats: an uneasy alliance and unfinished worship, verses 1 to 3; Solomon's prayer for an understanding heart at Gibeon, verses 4 to 9; God's answer of wisdom, riches, and honor, verses 10 to 15; and Solomon's judgment between the two women, verses 16 to 28.

verses.text: Later, Solomon formed an alliance with Pharaoh king of Egypt by marrying his daughter. Solomon brought her to the City of David until he had finished building his palace and the house of the LORD, as well as the wall around Jerusalem.
The people, however, were still sacrificing on the high places because a {{bayit|house}} for the Name of the LORD had not yet been built.
And Solomon loved the LORD and walked in the statutes of his father David, except that he sacrificed and burned incense on the high places.

units.label: Solomon's prayer for an understanding heart
units.note: ask, and I will give it to you

verses.text: Now the king went to Gibeon to sacrifice there, for it was the great high place. Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings on the altar there.
One night at Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream, and God said, “Ask, and I will give it to you!”

Solomon replied, “You have shown much {{chesed|loving devotion}} to Your servant, my father David, because he walked before You in faithfulness, righteousness, and uprightness of heart. And You have maintained this loving devotion by giving him a son to sit on his throne this very day.
And now, O LORD my God, You have made Your servant king in my father David’s place. But I am only a little child, not knowing how to go out or come in.
Your servant is here among the people You have chosen, a people too numerous to count or number.
Therefore give Your servant an understanding {{lev|heart}} to judge Your people and to discern between good and evil. For who is able to govern this great people of Yours?”

units.label: God's answer: wisdom, riches, and honor

verses.text: Now it pleased the Lord that Solomon had made this request.

So God said to him, “Since you have asked for this instead of requesting long life or wealth for yourself or death for your enemies, but you have asked for discernment to administer {{mishpat|justice}},
behold, I will do what you have asked. I will give you a wise and discerning heart, so that there will never have been another like you, nor will there ever be.
Moreover, I will give you what you did not request, both riches and honor, so that during all your days no man in any kingdom will be your equal.
So if you walk in My ways and keep My statutes and commandments, just as your father David did, I will prolong your days.”

Then Solomon awoke, and indeed it had been a dream. So he returned to Jerusalem, stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Then he held a feast for all his servants.

units.label: Solomon judges wisely
units.note: the wisdom of God was in him

verses.text: At that time two prostitutes came to the king and stood before him.
One woman said, “Please, my lord, this woman and I live in the same house, and I gave birth while she was in the house.
On the third day after I gave birth, this woman also had a baby. We were alone, with no one in the house but the two of us.
During the night this woman’s son died because she rolled over on him.
So she got up in the middle of the night and took my son from my side while I was asleep. She laid him in her bosom and put her dead son at my bosom.
The next morning, when I got up to nurse my son, I discovered he was dead. But when I examined him, I realized that he was not the son I had borne.”

“No,” said the other woman, “the living one is my son and the dead one is your son.” But the first woman insisted, “No, the dead one is yours and the living one is mine.” So they argued before the king.
Then the king replied, “This woman says, ‘My son is alive and yours is dead,’ but that woman says, ‘No, your son is dead and mine is alive.’”
The king continued, “Bring me a sword.” So they brought him a sword,
and the king declared, “Cut the living child in two and give half to one and half to the other.”

Then the woman whose son was alive spoke to the king because she yearned with compassion for her son. “Please, my lord,” she said, “give her the living baby. Do not kill him!” But the other woman said, “He will be neither mine nor yours. Cut him in two!”
Then the king gave his ruling: “Give the living baby to the first woman. By no means should you kill him; she is his mother.”
When all Israel heard of the judgment the king had given, they stood in awe of him, for they saw that the {{chokmah|wisdom}} of God was in him to administer {{mishpat|justice}}.

bayit.gloss: House, here specifically the house, or temple, for the Name of the LORD, not yet built when Solomon prays at Gibeon. The people still sacrifice at scattered high places because this bayit does not yet exist; its construction, filling the chapters ahead, will finally give Israel one central place to meet with God, a shadow of the true dwelling of God with His people fulfilled in Christ, John 1:14, and in the church, His body, 1 Corinthians 3:16.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant kindness. Solomon opens his prayer by recounting the chesed the LORD has already shown his father David, and the chesed maintained in giving David a son to sit on his throne. His request for wisdom grows directly out of gratitude for grace already received, not merit he has earned.

lev.gloss: Heart, the seat of understanding, discernment, and moral character. Solomon asks for an understanding lev to judge God's people rightly, echoing 1 Samuel 16:7, where the LORD looks on the lev rather than outward appearance. Wisdom, in Scripture, is finally a matter of the heart, not merely the intellect.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of a case or a society according to God's standards. Solomon asks for discernment to administer mishpat, and the chapter closes by showing that very mishpat displayed before all Israel in the case of the two women, proof that the LORD had granted exactly what Solomon requested.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skill in living rightly before God and administering His justice well. This is the chapter's central word: it is what Solomon asks for above riches or long life, what God grants beyond all others, and what all Israel finally recognizes at work in him, 'the chokmah of God was in him.' Christ is greater than Solomon and is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30, Matthew 12:42.

basic.context: Chapter 3 opens with a note of compromise, an alliance sealed by marriage to Pharaoh's daughter and worship still offered at the high places since the temple is not yet built. At Gibeon the LORD appears to Solomon in a dream and offers him anything he asks; Solomon requests an understanding heart to govern God's people rather than long life, wealth, or the death of his enemies. God is pleased and grants the request, adding riches and honor Solomon did not ask for. The chapter closes with Solomon's famous ruling between two women disputing a living child, a judgment that leaves all Israel in awe at the wisdom of God at work in him. The key questions are what Solomon's request reveals about true wisdom, why God gives so generously beyond what was asked, and how the case of the two women demonstrates that wisdom in action.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a real dream encounter and a real legal case, not a formula guaranteeing that anyone who asks for wisdom receives supernatural insight in the same measure. What the chapter teaches as an enduring truth is that God delights in requests shaped by love for others rather than self-interest, and that true wisdom flows from a heart rightly ordered toward Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The people still lack a bayit, a house, for the LORD's Name, so Solomon worships at Gibeon instead. There he asks for an understanding lev, heart, to govern well, and God grants him chokmah, wisdom, beyond any other, along with the mishpat, justice, that Israel then sees displayed in his famous ruling.
basic.typology: Solomon's request for wisdom rather than riches or long life anticipates Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. God's generosity in giving Solomon more than he asked pictures the Father's pattern of giving immeasurably more than we ask or imagine. And Solomon's discerning judgment, exposing the true mother's heart, points to Christ, who perfectly discerns the true condition of every heart.
basic.christ: Solomon asks for wisdom to govern rightly and receives it as an unearned gift; Jesus is greater than Solomon, and is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:30, Matthew 12:42. Where Solomon's wisdom was given and could later be compromised, Christ's wisdom is perfect, unfailing, and freely offered to all who ask, James 1:5.
basic.god: God delights in prayers shaped by love for others rather than self-interest, and He gives generously, often far beyond what is asked. He is also the God of true justice, whose wisdom is displayed not in abstract theory but in real, costly discernment applied to real human need.
basic.crossReferences: James 1:5 promises that God gives wisdom generously to all who ask. 1 Kings 4:29-34 describes the astonishing breadth of the wisdom God gave Solomon. Matthew 12:42 records Jesus declaring that something greater than Solomon is here. And 1 Corinthians 1:30 names Christ Himself as our wisdom from God.
basic.oneStory: Israel now has a king, but not yet a temple, and in that unfinished season God meets Solomon in a dream and gives him what he asked, wisdom to govern well. That wisdom is displayed immediately and becomes the hallmark of Solomon's early reign, a foretaste of the true wisdom of God finally and fully revealed in Jesus, greater than Solomon, in whom all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden.

context.literary: The chapter is framed by contrast: it opens with Solomon's compromise, marriage alliance and worship at the high places, and closes with Solomon's shining public wisdom, so that the reader sees both his weakness and his God-given strength side by side from the very start of his reign. The dream scene at the center unfolds as a dialogue of remarkable humility, and the courtroom scene that follows dramatizes, in vivid narrative form, exactly the wisdom just granted.
context.historical: Marriage alliances with foreign royal houses were standard diplomacy in the ancient Near East, though Israel's own law warned against marriages that would turn the heart toward foreign gods, Deuteronomy 17:17. Gibeon, called 'the great high place,' housed the tabernacle and bronze altar at this time, 1 Chronicles 16:39, 21:29, so Solomon's worship there, though at a high place, was not necessarily pagan syncretism. Royal courts in the ancient world were expected to render final judgment in difficult legal cases, and the case of two women each claiming a single living child, with no witnesses, was exactly the kind of dispute only a wise ruler could resolve.
context.canonical: This chapter launches the account of Solomon's reign proper, and the wisdom granted here becomes the foundation for the wealth, building projects, and international renown described in the chapters that follow, culminating in the building of the temple. It also plants an early seed, worship at the high places, that will resurface as a genuine problem later in Solomon's life, 1 Kings 11:7-8, showing that even great grace does not eliminate the ongoing need for full obedience.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that true wisdom is a gift God delights to give to those who ask for it out of love for others rather than self-interest, and to demonstrate that gift immediately at work in a real act of costly, discerning justice that leaves all Israel in awe.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical dream and a specific legal ruling; it does not prescribe dreams as the normal means of receiving wisdom today, nor does it prescribe threatening violence as a method of legal discovery. What endures is the pattern that God gives wisdom generously to those who ask rightly, and that wisdom shows itself in real, practical justice and compassion.

debates.question: Was Solomon's sacrificing at the high places, including at Gibeon, a sinful compromise or an acceptable practice for his time?

views.label: Acceptable given the historical moment
views.case: The text itself explains the people did this only because the temple was not yet built, verse 2, softening any note of blame, and Gibeon specifically is called 'the great high place' where the tabernacle and bronze altar were then located, 1 Chronicles 16:39, 21:29, so Solomon's worship there was not necessarily pagan syncretism.

views.label: A real, if minor, compromise
views.case: The text still records it as an exception to Solomon's love for the LORD, 'except that he sacrificed... on the high places,' verse 3, and the high places later become a major source of idolatry in Israel's history, so this note plants an early seed of the same compromise that will eventually corrupt Solomon's reign, 1 Kings 11:7-8.

debates.bearing: Both readings recognize this as a provisional arrangement before the temple's completion, and both see the same pattern that will resurface later in Solomon's life, where partial obedience opens a door that later becomes a snare.

debates.question: Was Solomon's wisdom purely a gift of grace, or was it also his own genuine moral achievement?

views.label: An unearned gift of grace
views.case: Solomon receives what he did not merit, granted solely because God delighted in his request, verses 10 and 12, consistent with wisdom, like every good gift, ultimately coming down from the Father, James 1:5, 17.

views.label: A response commended for its rightly ordered desire
views.case: Though a gift, the chapter also commends Solomon's asking itself as praiseworthy, since he could have selfishly requested long life, wealth, or vengeance and did not; his humility and love for God's people in making this particular request is itself presented as wisdom already at work, being rewarded and enlarged.

debates.bearing: Together these hold what Scripture holds elsewhere, that wisdom is God's gift, yet its right use and desire in a person, however imperfect, is genuinely commendable and precious in His sight, without contradicting grace.

study.originalLanguages: The people lack a bayit, a house, for the LORD's Name, worshiping instead at scattered high places until the temple can be built. In his prayer Solomon asks for an understanding lev, heart, echoing the LORD's own words to Samuel that He looks on the lev rather than outward appearance, 1 Samuel 16:7. God grants chokmah, wisdom, beyond any other, precisely the mishpat, discernment for justice, Solomon requested, and the chapter closes by showing that same mishpat vindicated before all Israel in the case of the two women.

typology.type: Solomon asking not for riches or long life but for wisdom to govern God's people rightly
typology.fulfillment: Christ, greater than Solomon, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3, and who is Himself our wisdom from God, 1 Corinthians 1:30.

typology.type: God granting Solomon's request and adding far more than he asked
typology.fulfillment: God's pattern of giving immeasurably more than we ask or imagine, Ephesians 3:20, ultimately fulfilled in the gift of His own Son.

typology.type: Solomon's wise judgment exposing the true mother by testing what each woman was willing to sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who discerns and judges the true condition of every heart, Hebrews 4:12-13, and of whom Jesus said something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42.

study.christ: Solomon asks God for wisdom rather than riches, long life, or vengeance, and God delights to grant it along with what he did not request. Jesus is greater than Solomon in exactly this: He is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30, and in Him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3. Where Solomon's wisdom, however great, would later be compromised by his own divided heart, Christ's wisdom is perfect and unfailing, freely given to all who ask in faith, James 1:5, and Jesus Himself declares that something, someone, greater than Solomon has now come, Matthew 12:42.
study.god: God delights in requests shaped by love for His people rather than self-interest, and He answers such prayers with striking generosity, giving Solomon not only what he asked but riches and honor besides. He is also the God of true justice, whose wisdom is not abstract but shows itself in real, costly discernment applied to a desperate, seemingly unsolvable human dispute, vindicating the truth and protecting the vulnerable.

crossReferences.note: God gives wisdom generously and without finding fault to all who ask Him, the same generosity Solomon experiences at Gibeon.
The breadth and fame of the wisdom God gave Solomon, exceeding all the wisdom of the East and of Egypt.
Jesus declares that something greater than Solomon is here, identifying Himself as the wisdom Solomon's reign only foreshadowed.
Christ is described as the wisdom of God, and as the one who became wisdom from God for those who believe.
The LORD gives wisdom; from His mouth come knowledge and understanding, the very pattern Solomon experiences in this chapter.

study.oneStory: Israel now has a king but not yet a temple, and into that unfinished season God meets Solomon in a dream and grants exactly what he asks, wisdom to govern God's people well, along with riches and honor he did not request. That wisdom is put on immediate, public display in the case of the two women, establishing Solomon's early reign as a season when the wisdom of God was visibly at work among His people, a foretaste of the true and greater wisdom of God finally revealed in Jesus Christ, in whom every treasure of wisdom and knowledge is hidden.

sermon.title: A King's Wisdom, Solomon
sermon.why: Preached from 1 Kings 3:16-28 as part of the series Pointers to Christ, Keller examines the need for wisdom, the anatomy of wisdom, and the heart of wisdom running through Solomon's judgment, directly expounding this chapter's climactic scene.

quiz.q: What did Solomon ask God for at Gibeon, and what did he deliberately not ask for?

options.text: He asked for an understanding heart to discern good and evil and govern God's people rightly, deliberately not asking for long life, wealth, or the death of his enemies, v9, 11.
options.note: Verses 9 and 11 together state exactly this request and its deliberate exclusions.

options.text: He asked to be a good king like his father David, without making any specific request.
options.note: Too vague; the prayer contains a specific, named request, an understanding heart to discern and to judge.

options.text: He asked mainly for wealth and honor so that his reign would be secure.
options.note: The opposite of what he requested; wealth and honor were explicitly what he did not ask for, though God granted them anyway, verse 13.

options.text: He asked for victory over all his enemies.
options.note: Explicitly not requested and contrary to verse 11, which lists this among the things Solomon did not ask for.

quiz.q: Why did it please the Lord that Solomon made this request, verses 10-12?

options.text: Because Solomon asked for discernment to administer justice rather than for personal gain, self-preservation, or vengeance.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly contrasts Solomon's actual request with the self-serving requests he could have made.

options.text: Because Solomon prayed at all, simply showing that he still trusted God despite his youth.
options.note: True but partial; it understates the specific, others-focused content of the request that the text highlights.

options.text: Because Solomon promised to build the temple immediately as part of his prayer.
options.note: Not part of the recorded prayer at Gibeon; the temple's construction is addressed in later chapters.

options.text: Because Solomon asked for something small and unimportant.
options.note: Contrary to the text, which repeatedly treats this request as significant and worthy of God's pleasure.

quiz.q: What did God give Solomon beyond what he requested?

options.text: Riches and honor, so that no king in his lifetime would equal him, in addition to the wise and discerning heart he had asked for.
options.note: Verses 12-13 state this precisely.

options.text: Only long life, since that is what most kings desire above all else.
options.note: Long life is mentioned in verse 14, but it is conditional on obedience and is not the primary unrequested gift highlighted in verses 12-13.

options.text: Nothing further; God gave Solomon exactly what he asked and no more.
options.note: Contrary to verse 13, which explicitly states God gave what Solomon did not request.

options.text: Victory over Egypt as a reward for his faithfulness.
options.note: Not in the text, and Solomon had just made a marriage alliance with Egypt, verse 1, not fought against it.

quiz.q: How does Solomon's judgment between the two women reveal true wisdom, verses 16-27?

options.text: By proposing to divide the living child, Solomon exposes which woman truly loves the child by her willingness to give him up rather than see him harmed.
options.note: Verses 26-27 show the test revealing the true mother's compassion, which becomes the basis for Solomon's ruling.

options.text: By carefully cross-examining both women's stories for factual inconsistencies.
options.note: The text shows no cross-examination of details; the decisive test is the proposal to divide the child, not detective work on their stories.

options.text: By ruling in favor of whichever woman spoke first and most confidently.
options.note: The ruling depends on the compassion revealed under the sword test, not on who spoke first or most confidently.

options.text: By actually dividing the child in two as he first proposed.
options.note: That proposal was a test, not the actual judgment; verse 27 shows the living child given whole to his true mother.

quiz.q: What was Israel's response to Solomon's ruling, and what did the narrator say it proved?

options.text: All Israel stood in awe of the king because they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to administer justice.
options.note: This is the exact closing statement of the chapter, verse 28.

options.text: The two women reconciled with each other and became close friends.
options.note: Not stated anywhere in the text; the narrative closes with Israel's response to Solomon, not the women's relationship.

options.text: The people demanded that a formal written law be created based on the case.
options.note: Not mentioned in the text.

options.text: The people were unimpressed, believing the ruling was merely a lucky guess.
options.note: The opposite of verse 28, which says the people stood in awe, recognizing God's own wisdom at work.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: Solomon's wisdom, sought and given as a gift rather than earned, anticipates Christ, who is Himself the wisdom of God and greater than Solomon.
options.note: Directly supported by 1 Corinthians 1:30 and Jesus's own words in Matthew 12:42.

options.text: It shows that good kings always ask God for help before making any decision.
options.note: True in a general sense, but it understates the specific, christological pointer the chapter contains.

options.text: It teaches that wisdom can only be gained through many years of hard experience.
options.note: Contrary to the chapter, where Solomon's wisdom is a sudden gift given while he is still, by his own admission, only 'a little child,' verse 7.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to Christ or the New Testament.
options.note: False; the connection is explicit in Jesus's own statement that something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42.

questions: Solomon could have asked for long life, wealth, or victory over his enemies, but asked instead for wisdom to serve others well. If God offered you anything right now, what would your request reveal about your heart?
God delighted in Solomon's request because it was shaped by love for God's people rather than self-interest. Where in your own praying do you notice self-interest crowding out love for others?
God gave Solomon far more than he asked for. Where have you seen God's generosity exceed what you dared to request, and how does that shape your confidence in bringing requests to Him now?
The chapter opens with Solomon's compromise at the high places even as it celebrates his wisdom. Where might you be tempted to accept partial obedience alongside genuine devotion to God?

Solomon's wisdom was tested and proven in a real, costly, human situation, not in the abstract. Where is God currently asking you to apply wisdom to a real and difficult situation rather than simply admiring wisdom from a distance?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote observes that 'the Lord' here and in verse 15 renders the Hebrew Adonai, distinct from the covenant name LORD, YHWH, used elsewhere in the chapter.

The narrator's note that the people, and even Solomon, sacrificed at the high places 'because a house for the Name of the LORD had not yet been built' softens the practice as a provisional necessity rather than deliberate idolatry, though it still plants an early seed that resurfaces later in Solomon's reign, 1 Kings 11:7-8.

The two women are identified as prostitutes, a detail that heightens the difficulty of the case, since neither had a husband or independent witness to support her claim, making Solomon's discernment all the more remarkable.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 3:1-28. Four em dashes in the source text, two each in verses 11 and 13, have been converted to commas so that no em dashes or en dashes remain anywhere in this file.
